metadata.split_role: Expanded red/yellow, blue-staining, red-pored, and caution-lane bolete records.

review_reason_audit.rule: Normalize explicit review signals into review_reasons/review_note and prefix notes for Needs Review list visibility.

review_pass_notes.rule: Clear stale image-only review only when at least two distinct real source-image URLs exist; keep one-photo and placeholder records in review.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended as a food target in this guide because of confusion with similar red/yellow blue-stainers.

mushroom_profile.summary: Frequently misidentified oak/hardwood bolete; red-brown cap, yellow pores that blue, and yellow stem flushed red below.

records.mushroom_card_note: Frequently misidentified oak/hardwood bolete; red-brown cap, yellow pores that blue, and yellow stem flushed red below.
records.field_identification: Red to reddish-brown cap, bright yellow young pores bruising blue, yellow stem flushed red over the lower half, moderate blue bruising, and oak/hardwood association. Compare with bicolor and sensibilis.

records.culinary_uses: Avoid in this guide. Red-pored blue-staining boletes are not beginner targets, and species boundaries are messy.

mushroom_profile.summary: Red/orange-pored, blue-staining bolete group; included for caution and comparison.

records.edibility_detail: Treat as not recommended; high confusion risk with toxic red-pored boletes.
records.field_identification: Red to orange pore surface, strong blue staining, reticulate or patterned stem, and hardwood association place it in the high-caution red-pored bolete lane.
records.mushroom_card_note: Lurid Bolete group: red/orange-pored, blue-staining bolete; not a beginner edible and not something to toss casually in a pan.
records.photo_patch_basis: exact slug match in uploaded mushrooms.json

records.culinary_uses: Not a food target in this guide; retained for identification and caution comparison.

mushroom_profile.summary: Neoboletus-type red/yellow-pored blue-staining bolete; high-caution comparison record.

records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food due to red-pored blue-staining confusion risk.
records.mushroom_card_note: Yellow/red-pored blue-staining Neoboletus-type record; keep in the caution lane until identity is rock solid.
records.field_identification: Check for yellow to red pore tones, rapid blue staining, yellow flesh, and Neoboletus-style red-pored bolete traits. This is a microscope/taxonomy-heavy neighborhood.

records.culinary_uses: Not a food target in this guide; retained for identification and caution comparison.

mushroom_profile.summary: Red-pored blue-staining Neoboletus comparison species; high caution.

records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food unless independently expert-confirmed; this guide keeps it as a caution record.
records.mushroom_card_note: Red-pored blue-staining Neoboletus comparison species; useful mainly for keeping red-pored boletes out of the easy-edible bucket.
records.field_identification: Look for red/orange pore surface, blue bruising, yellowish flesh, and red-pored bolete build. Treat name-level ID conservatively.

records.culinary_uses: Do not treat as edible in this guide.

mushroom_profile.summary: Rubroboletus-style red-pored bolete with blue staining; avoid/comparison record.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September; treated conservatively with other eastern red-pored/blue-staining boletes until Upper Michigan timing is better documented.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.

records.mushroom_card_note: Perfumed Bolete / Rubroboletus-type caution species: red tones, blue staining, and toxicity/look-alike concern.
records.field_identification: Red to rosy cap/stem/pore elements, blue staining, and Rubroboletus-style red-pored features put this in the avoid/comparison lane.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid. Red-pored Rubroboletus-style boletes include toxic species and require expert-level ID.

mushroom_profile.summary: Caloboletus-style caution record; useful for comparison with edible boletes, not a table species.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September; treated conservatively with other eastern red-pored/blue-staining boletes until Upper Michigan timing is better documented.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.

records.mushroom_card_note: Piedmont Bolete: Caloboletus-style caution species; included for comparison, not for the skillet.
records.field_identification: Caloboletus records often involve pale to yellow pore surfaces, reticulate stems, bitter or unpleasant taste, and confusion with edible boletes; treat this one conservatively.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid unless independently confirmed; kept as a caution/comparison record.
records.photo_patch_basis: exact slug match in uploaded mushrooms.json

records.culinary_uses: Not a priority edible; if kept at all, use only young, fresh, confidently identified specimens and avoid old or moldy boletes.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Red Crack Bolete. Added in the Michigan audit pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
staining: Blue staining in cut or bruised flesh
mushroom_profile.season_note: Early summer into late fall / mid-winter in some broad references; for Upper Michigan treat as summer-fall pending local confirmation.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.

records.field_identification: Small pore-bearing bolete with a brownish dry/tomentose cap that often cracks to reveal reddish flesh below the cap skin. Yellow angular pores, olive-brown spore print, yellow stem with coral-red fibrils or red lower-stem tones, and blue staining in cut flesh help place it, but this species complex is often hard to separate without microscopy.

field_measurements.cap: Cap about 4–10 cm wide / about 1.5–4 in wide
field_measurements.stem: Stem about 4–13 cm long and 1–2 cm thick / about 1.6–5.1 in long and 0.4–0.8 in thick
field_measurements.notes: Several similar Xerocomellus/Hortiboletus taxa overlap; measurements support field sorting but do not prove species-level ID alone.

records.edibility_detail: Often listed as edible but not choice; do not rely on this group for food because similar cracked/red/yellow boletes are difficult and older specimens decay quickly.

records.culinary_uses: Do not collect for meals in this guide.
records.notes: Red/orange-pored bolete group with strong blue staining. Treat as a caution group in this guide; the red pore surface and fast bruising are the field marks that matter.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Red Mouth Bolete. Added in the Michigan / Upper Midwest bolete pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer, especially June through September in hardwoods.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.

records.general_notes: Use this entry as a warning bucket for red-pored, blue-staining boletes until individual species are confidently separated.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.field_identification: Brown to olive-brown cap, red to reddish-orange pores, yellow/red stem without strong reticulation, blue to blackish bruising, and velvety or fuzzy lower stem/base under hardwoods.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus subvelutipes group
use_links.notes: Red pores, blue staining, hardwood association, and fuzzy base traits.

records.edibility_notes: Brown-capped, red-pored, blue-staining boletes are a caution group. Treat as avoid unless a specific trusted species has been confirmed separately.

records.culinary_uses: Added as part of the red-pored / staining caution lane.
